one_liner: A 27-year-old man presents with acute asymmetric knee and ankle swelling, painful urination, and red itchy eyes that started about 3 weeks after an episode of bloody diarrhea on a camping trip.

stage2.hpi_extension: Three weeks ago, while camping, he developed 4 days of **bloody diarrhea, fever, and abdominal cramps** after eating undercooked chicken. That illness resolved on its own. Ten days ago he noticed **pain and swelling of the right knee and left ankle** and a **sausage-like swelling of his right third toe**. Over the last week he has developed **dysuria and mild urethral discharge** without a clear sexual exposure, and a **burning, red, gritty sensation in both eyes with mild photophobia**. He also notes a painless rash on the soles of his feet.
stage2.family_history: No known autoimmune or rheumatologic disease
stage2.social_history: Accountant. Recent camping trip where he ate undercooked chicken. Monogamous with same female partner for 2 years. Non-smoker. No IV drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: uncomfortable, limping. MSK: **warm, swollen, tender right knee with effusion**; **swollen tender left ankle**; **dactylitis ('sausage digit') of the right third toe**. Lumbar spine mildly tender over SI joints. Eyes: **bilateral conjunctival injection** without purulent discharge. GU: mild clear urethral discharge; **circinate balanitis** (painless serpiginous plaques on the glans). Skin: hyperkeratotic pustular plaques on the soles of the feet (**keratoderma blennorrhagicum**). Oral: small painless oral ulcer on the hard palate. Abdomen: soft, non-tender.

lab_results.result: **ESR 64 mm/hr**, **CRP 42 mg/L** (both elevated). WBC mildly elevated at 12.1.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated acute-phase reactants support an active inflammatory arthropathy following a recent infection.

lab_results.result: Stool culture: **Campylobacter jejuni** isolated (residual). Urethral nucleic acid amplification for Chlamydia and gonorrhea: **negative**. Knee arthrocentesis: cloudy fluid, WBC 18,000/uL with neutrophil predominance, **no crystals, Gram stain and culture negative**.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Inflammatory but sterile joint fluid following a documented enteric Campylobacter infection is the classic pattern of post-infectious reactive arthritis; crystals and septic arthritis are excluded.
HLA-B27 positivity is common in reactive arthritis and predicts more severe and chronic disease.

stage5.final_clue: NSAIDs and an intra-articular steroid injection into the right knee produce marked improvement. A short course of azithromycin is given empirically for the residual urethritis. At 6-week follow-up his joint symptoms and conjunctivitis have nearly resolved.

debrief.classic_presentation: Reactive arthritis is an **asymmetric oligoarthritis** that appears **1 to 4 weeks after** a triggering **GI infection (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia)** or **GU infection (Chlamydia trachomatis)**. The classic triad is **'Can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree'**  -  **conjunctivitis, urethritis, arthritis**. Other features include **dactylitis ('sausage digit')**, **enthesitis** (especially Achilles), **keratoderma blennorrhagicum** (hyperkeratotic foot plaques), **circinate balanitis**, and painless oral ulcers. Sacroiliitis may develop with chronic disease.
debrief.pathophysiology: Reactive arthritis is a post-infectious immune-mediated arthropathy in which bacterial antigens trigger a cross-reactive T-cell response in genetically susceptible hosts. It is strongly associated with **HLA-B27** and is part of the seronegative spondyloarthropathy family (PAIR: Psoriatic, Ankylosing spondylitis, IBD-associated, Reactive arthritis). The joint itself is sterile; organisms are not cultured from synovial fluid, though nucleic acid fragments may be present. RF and anti-CCP are negative.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line therapy is **NSAIDs** for joint symptoms. **Intra-articular corticosteroid injections** are helpful for large-joint monoarthritis. If a residual GU infection (Chlamydia) is identified, **antibiotics** (azithromycin or doxycycline) are given to the patient and sexual partners. Enteric pathogens generally do not require antibiotics at the time of arthritis diagnosis. Persistent disease may be treated with sulfasalazine or methotrexate; severe refractory cases respond to TNF inhibitors.
high_yield_pearls: Classic triad: Conjunctivitis + Urethritis + Arthritis ('Can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree').
Trigger = GI (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia) or GU (Chlamydia trachomatis) infection 1-4 weeks prior.
HLA-B27-associated seronegative spondyloarthropathy (PAIR); RF and anti-CCP negative.
Keratoderma blennorrhagicum on the soles and circinate balanitis on the glans are pathognomonic skin findings.
Synovial fluid is inflammatory but sterile  -  culture negative, no crystals; rules out septic arthritis and crystal arthropathy.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Seronegative spondyloarthropathies  -  Reactive arthritis' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'HLA-B27 (PAIR) associations'.
